_comment: Schema example for a Company Context data dict. Replace placeholder values with synthesized fields from a real client run. The skill protocol writes this file as `output/[slug]-data.json` during Phase 5; the build script consumes it to produce the HTML paste sheet.
value_proposition: One-paragraph value proposition pulled from the homepage hero, About page, and product page synthesis. First-person plural (we/our) when the client speaks in first person on their own site. Operational specificity over hype.
pain_points: One paragraph describing the operator's pain and how the client addresses it. Pull verbatim site language where possible.
Same shape. Aim for 3–5 pain points covering the breadth of the client's offering.
Each pain point should map to a real product/service or capability the client offers.

products.title: Category 1: [Product or Service Category Name]
products.models: Specific model numbers, line names, or SKU families if applicable
products.blurb: Operational description of the category — what's in it, who buys it, key specs. Match the depth a sales engineer would write, not a homepage hero.

personality_note: All 4 picks must come from HubSpot's Personality picklist (see references/hubspot-company-context-fields.md). Common LLM-invented picks like 'Knowledgeable' or 'Direct' are NOT in the picklist.
default_tone_note: All 4 picks must come from HubSpot's Default Tone picklist. Note that 'Direct' is NOT in the picklist — closest analogues are Straightforward, Matter-of-fact, Assertive.
mission: One paragraph mission statement, capped at 50 words. Pull from the About page or footer if present; otherwise synthesize from value-prop signals.
terms_note: Word count: count up to 20 words. Add notes about why specific terms are excluded (e.g., 'X is intentionally NOT on this list because it's in the brand tagline').
replacement_note: Notes on when these rules should fire (e.g., speech-to-text artifacts from internal calls render the brand as X; auto-correct to Y).

industry_classification.note: Title Case override of any Breeze ALL_CAPS_UNDERSCORE format
Reflect what the company DOES, not what their customers sell
Common values: Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Industrials, Technology, etc.

positive_associations: Strong reputation marker pulled from testimonials, reviews, About-page language
Operational competency marker (years in business, geographic depth, specialization)
negative_associations_note: No significant negative associations found in public web as of [Month YYYY]. Surfaces checked: general web search, Trustpilot, Yelp, BBB-style listings, industry trade press. Caveats: Reddit subforums, Glassdoor, LinkedIn employee posts, BBB direct, and Google Business reviews are not deeply covered by automated WebSearch — for full confidence, manual scan of these surfaces would close the gap.

competitors.name: Competitor One Name
competitors.note: Why they compete. Territory or category overlap. Any flag (also-customer, also-partner) if applicable.

competitors_note: Researched against [industry directory] + web search [Month YYYY]. Validated against any client-provided competitor knowledge. Flag any competitive lanes (e.g., 'rep-side competitors compete on manufacturer specs; distributor-side competitors compete on operator fulfillment').
content_topics: Topic 1 the client publishes about regularly
tech_note: Notes on tech stack peculiarities, integrations, custom-built tools.
social_causes: What community/social initiatives the client supports. None explicit on the website is a valid finding.
sustainability: Sustainability claims or initiatives. None explicit is a valid finding.
open_items: Inclusivity preferences — confirm the 3 toggle settings (gender-neutral, cultural, idioms)
Picklist validations — flag any picklist values that may need closest-fit override against HubSpot's actual UI
Replacement rules scope — confirm where auto-correction should fire (drafts, publish, both)
Anything specific to this client — open data gaps the client must close
